template.name: demo-dogfood
template.description: Lunastak strategy coach demo - complete conversation with extraction and knowledge summary

projects.name: My Strategy
projects.knowledgeSummary:
You're building Luna as a continuous strategic companion for leaders drowning in reactive mode—those founders and executives at growth-stage companies (8-15M ARR, 60-150 staff) who have great strategic instincts but struggle to clarify and operationalize them. You've identified a critical insight: executives never need a new strategy, they need help clarifying the muddy one they already have. The chaos of constant firefighting, shifting priorities, and being the organizational bottleneck creates a "clarity crisis" that looks like execution problems but is actually strategic vagueness.

Luna democratizes what McKinsey partners provide—being there for decision coaching, outside perspective, and making strategy actionable—but continuously and affordably rather than in expensive, delayed engagements. You're positioning this as a "longitudinal strategy coach" rather than one-off deck generators like Xavier AI or execution-only platforms like WorkBoard. Leaders can drop in snippets and chaos anytime, and Luna helps wrangle it into coherent strategy that bridges from annual offsites through quarterly planning to monthly business reviews.

Your evolution path is clear: start with individual leaders preparing for strategy moments, then expand to multi-user platforms that track strategy evolution and automate 75% of business reviews. You're combining strategic frameworks (7 Powers, Porter's 5 Forces) with execution methodologies (OKRs) and backing the AI with veteran strategy consultants—expert-augmented rather than pure automation.

The market gap is real: no one combines deep strategic elicitation, continuous coaching, and operational integration for this underserved segment.
suggestedQuestions: How will you balance being accessible enough for reactive leaders to actually use Luna in chaotic moments, while still delivering the depth of strategic insight that distinguishes you from simpler AI tools?

What's your hypothesis about why growth-stage leaders would choose Luna over just hiring a fractional Chief Strategy Officer or part-time consultant—especially given the "expert-backed" positioning suggests human expertise matters?

As Luna evolves from single-user tool to multi-user organizational platform, how do you prevent it from becoming just another "strategy software" that leadership mandates but teams ignore—the very "decoration" problem you identified?

messages.content: Hey! I remember we've mapped out quite a bit of your strategic landscape together. I'm curious – as you've been thinking about this "longitudinal strategy coach" positioning versus the one-off deck generators, have you started to form any hypotheses about what the actual cadence or rhythm of engagement would look like for your users? Like, what does "longitudinal" really mean in practice for a growth-stage leader trying to execute their strategy?

Yes! Strategy and planning comes around a few times a year and it's always a huge scramble. But it's a scramble because leaders often aren't managing strategy and operations in a proactive way. To be fair, it's not their fault, they don't have time. 100% in reactive mode. 

Lunastak takes away some of this crazy. Leaders don't have to "make time" for strategy. Instead, just drop snippets, parts, memos, or just random and seemingly unconnected information into Luna, and... forget about it. Until your ready to do somethign with it. When you get back, Luna's made some sense of nuttiness. She won't have solved it, but she's there, ready to help you solve it—joining the dots on your behalf; asking the right questions; distilling and capturing the essence of yoru thinking. 

When Luna gets that right, there's immediate value, and so long as we keep the tap on, then leaders can engage as often and for as long as they like. 

The other aspect of "longitudinal" is beyond just the planning cycles. Everyone know that getting the strategy done is just the beginning, and execution is everything. This is where Luna can really shine. Using OKRs to measure performance? Terrific, completely compatible—manage those in Luna. Operating model requires Monthly and Quarterly Business Review? Fabulous! A 75% draft of that document can be automated from Luna—your contribution is the polished narrative.

I love how Luna becomes this continuous strategic companion rather than just another planning tool! The image of leaders dropping in "snippets and seemingly unconnected information" really resonates—that's exactly how strategic thinking actually happens in the wild.

Quick question: when you picture the leader who's going to get the most value from this longitudinal approach—the one who's constantly in reactive mode, juggling strategy fragments—what does their world look like? Are we talking about founders at high-growth startups, VP-level executives at mid-market SaaS companies, or someone else entirely? I'm curious both about the company stage/size and what their day-to-day chaos actually consists of.

It's all of those, and there's much precedence in our consulting work for helping those archetypes. Pragmatically though, it's for any leader who's needs a collaborative parter to wrangle strategy with them. This is the hard part of strategy... Anyone can turn up the the annual offsite and dial up the charisma to 11. Everyone's juiced. It feels good. You could say... anything! But what happens when the McKinsey partners are gone? 8 months later, when all you can remember is the sweet taste of aspiration—before reality punched you in the face? They'll be there for you, but by the time you schedule the meeting and get lunch together, your two-weeks out, and  it's too late. 

Luna is strategy partner that's always there, always thinking, and always ready to move you forward, exactly when you need it the most.
messages.confidenceReasoning: The conversation has strong coverage of the problem (reactive strategy management, execution gap) and value proposition (continuous strategic companion vs. one-off tools), with vivid use cases around planning cycles and execution support. However, customer definition remains broad ("any leader who needs a collaborative partner"), and competitive/market context is only implied through contrast with consultants and "deck generators" - there's enough specificity to generate meaningful strategy output while clearly identifying areas needing deeper exploration (target segment prioritization, competitive landscape, pricing/business model).

messages.content: The "8 months later when all you can remember is the sweet taste of aspiration" moment is so visceral—I can practically feel that panic. But here's what I'm wondering: when a leader is in that moment of realizing their strategy has drifted or stalled, what's typically stopping them from course-correcting? Is it that they don't know *what* to do differently, they don't have the *data* to make informed pivots, they can't get their *team* aligned on changes, or is it something else entirely? I'm trying to understand what specific capability Luna needs to deliver in those critical "reality punch" moments to actually move them forward versus just being another source of guilt about what they should be doing.

All of those. Clarity is huge. Every executive I work with—they NEVER need (or want) a new strategy—they just need help clarifying the one they have. And that's the thing, it's muddy, murky, and mixed up with all of the other chaos they're dealing with. 

Sometimes is "decision coaching". They're stuck choosing between 10 great options. The instinct is to do it all... but that usually doesn't go well. Or it's making the strategy actionable. It'll work! If only they could describe the future in ways that motivate, and orchestrate teams toward coherent action. Sometimes it's forest for the trees, and it's the outside-in perspective that can unlock that.
messages.confidenceReasoning: The conversation has strong coverage across all key dimensions: clear customer archetype (growth-stage leaders in reactive mode), specific problem (strategy clarity and execution continuity between planning cycles), and differentiated value proposition (always-on strategic partner vs. episodic consultants/tools). There's sufficient concrete detail about the use case, user behavior patterns, and specific capabilities needed to generate meaningful strategy while also identifying areas for further exploration (exact ICP prioritization, competitive positioning against existing tools).

messages.content: You've painted this really rich picture of the *types* of strategic bottlenecks Luna helps with—clarity, decision paralysis, making it actionable, outside perspective. But I'm curious: when you think about your consulting work with these leaders, what's the pattern in how they actually *use* you during those moments? Like, are they coming to you with a specific artifact they need help with (board deck, OKR refresh, team offsite prep), or is it more freeform "I'm stuck and need to talk this through" conversations? I'm trying to understand what the actual interaction model should feel like when someone opens Luna in one of those critical moments—what are they hoping to *do* versus just *discuss*?

Different strokes for different folks. But here's the most common scenarios I see:
- They think the strategy is fine (amazing even), but the team isn't up to the task. Actual truth: The team is good and capable, but the strategy is vague, direction changes with the wind, and nobody has a clue what matters—so how could they possibly deliver it? That's a leadership problem. 
- There's great strategy. It's aspirational, well researched, practical, logical, feasible! But the posters on the wall about company culture are decorations at best, meanwhile, teams are living in feature factory that dialled up to 11 on velocity, where everyone's completely lost sight of what actually matters (too busy tryna 'make the number'). P.s. It's usually an OKR. 
- Total bedlam. It's a founder led org who's grown into success. The founders are used to making every decision, and pride themselves on being capable of doing so. And they are. Except that they aren't—not at scale. They become the bottle neck. Can't make decisions fast enough. Can be across all the detail. They're exhausted. They're carrying ALL the responsibility. How they worked before (at small scale) isn't working at their current scale. People need more, and expect more, but they're tapped out and burned out. 

All of those archetypes benefit from the same types of help.
Chaos reduction. Clarity. Help doing the lonely heavy lifting of strategic leadership. At moments when they know they're hard to be around and hard to tolerate. No wonder partners at big consulting cost so much! This is their job—but most people can't afford that luxury.
messages.confidenceReasoning: The conversation has substantial coverage across all key dimensions: clear customer archetypes (growth-stage leaders in reactive mode), specific problem understanding (strategy drift, clarity gaps, decision paralysis), and strong differentiation (longitudinal vs. one-off, always-available vs. expensive consultants). There's enough concrete detail about use cases, user behaviors, and value delivery moments to generate meaningful strategy output while also identifying areas for further exploration (like specific interaction models and initial customer segment prioritization).

messages.content: I have what I need to create your strategy.

You nailed it. Those are common reasons. Feeling exposed about annual board meeting and nothing new to say since last year. A steady hand who's critical to the business just left, and things are barely holding together. Investors. Generally just missing more than hitting. Big important initiatives chewing resources but seemingly not going in the right direction. Overwhelm of tryna lead a division/company, while drowning in the detail of everyday decisions, and the expectations of employees.
messages.confidenceReasoning: The conversation has strong coverage across all key dimensions: clear customer archetypes (growth-stage leaders in reactive mode), specific problem understanding (strategy clarity, execution gap, decision paralysis), and concrete value proposition (continuous strategic companion vs. one-off consulting). The user has provided rich, specific scenarios with enough detail to generate meaningful strategy while also revealing natural areas for development (exact ICP prioritization, pricing model for longitudinal engagement).

messages.content: I have what I need to create your strategy.

themes.content: Luna is positioned as a "longitudinal strategy coach" rather than a one-off deck generator. The core insight is that strategy isn't a discrete event but an ongoing process. Leaders can drop "snippets, parts, memos, or just random and seemingly unconnected information" into Luna continuously, and she helps make sense of the chaos over time. This contrasts with traditional consulting (McKinsey partners who leave after the offsite) or static planning tools. Luna is "always there, always thinking, and always ready to move you forward" - available in the critical moments 8 months after the offsite when "all you can remember is the sweet taste of aspiration before reality punched you in the face."
themes.theme_name: Continuous Strategic Companion vs. Point-in-Time Tools

themes.content: The conversation identified that "getting the strategy done is just the beginning, and execution is everything." Luna bridges the gap between planning cycles and ongoing execution through integration with operational cadences. This includes compatibility with OKRs for performance measurement and automation of 75% of Monthly and Quarterly Business Reviews, leaving leaders to focus on "the polished narrative." The value isn't just in creating strategy documents but in maintaining strategic coherence through the messy reality of execution over time.
themes.theme_name: Strategy Execution Gap: Planning to Performance

themes.content: The target user is characterized by being "100% in reactive mode" - not because they're incompetent but because "they don't have time" to manage strategy proactively. Strategy and planning "comes around a few times a year and it's always a huge scramble." These leaders are juggling chaos, drowning in everyday decisions, dealing with employee expectations, and often become bottlenecks as organizations scale. They include founders at high-growth startups, VP-level executives at mid-market companies, and division leaders - essentially "any leader who needs a collaborative partner to wrangle strategy with them."

A critical insight emerged: executives "NEVER need (or want) a new strategy—they just need help clarifying the one they have." The strategy is "muddy, murky, and mixed up with all of the other chaos they're dealing with." Common manifestations include: teams thinking strategy is fine but execution is failing (actually the strategy is vague and direction changes constantly); having great strategy on paper but teams lost in "feature factory" velocity; and decision paralysis when choosing between "10 great options" with the instinct to "do it all." Luna's role is "chaos reduction" and helping with "the lonely heavy lifting of strategic leadership."
themes.theme_name: Clarity as the Core Strategic Bottleneck

themes.content: The conversation surfaced specific recurring scenarios: (1) Leaders blame team capability when actually the strategy is vague and priorities shift constantly - "that's a leadership problem"; (2) Great strategy exists but culture posters are "decorations" while teams are "completely lost sight of what actually matters"; (3) Founder-led orgs where founders become bottlenecks at scale, "exhausted" and "carrying ALL the responsibility" because "how they worked before isn't working at their current scale." Additional triggers include feeling exposed at board meetings with nothing new to say, key people leaving, missing more than hitting, and big initiatives "chewing resources but seemingly not going in the right direction."

Luna addresses an accessibility gap in strategic support. The conversation highlighted that consulting partners at firms like McKinsey provide exactly this type of help - being there in critical moments, providing outside perspective, decision coaching, making strategy actionable - but "most people can't afford that luxury." By the time you "schedule the meeting and get lunch together, you're two weeks out, and it's too late." Luna makes this level of strategic partnership available continuously and affordably, serving as the "steady hand" that expensive consultants provide but at a fraction of the cost and with immediate availability.

The interaction model is deliberately flexible - "different strokes for different folks." Leaders engage with Luna both through specific artifacts (board decks, OKR refreshes, team offsite prep, Monthly/Quarterly Business Reviews) and freeform strategic thinking. The key is that leaders can "engage as often and for as long as they like" with the "tap on" for continuous value. Luna provides multiple types of support: decision coaching when stuck between options, making strategy actionable and motivating, providing outside-in perspective for "forest for the trees" moments, and "joining the dots" on seemingly unconnected information.
themes.theme_name: Flexible Engagement Model: Artifacts and Conversations

emerging: The interaction model is taking shape—leaders need both artifact creation (board decks, OKR refreshes, QBR drafts) AND freeform thinking partnership. The "75% automated draft" concept for QBRs suggests a compelling balance between AI assistance and human narrative polish, but the exact choreography of when Luna proactively surfaces insights versus waits for prompting could be further defined.

The "different strokes for different folks" acknowledgment hints at potential segmentation (founder-led vs. division leader vs. VP-level), but the core insight is that they all need the same fundamental help: chaos reduction, clarity, and a thinking partner during the lonely heavy lifting. This suggests a flexible engagement model rather than rigid personas.
strengths: The "continuous strategic companion" model is powerfully differentiated—leaders drop in fragments continuously rather than scrambling during planning cycles, and Luna helps them make sense of chaos without requiring them to "make time" for strategy. This directly addresses the reactive mode problem.

The value proposition is crystal clear across the full strategy lifecycle: from clarifying muddy thinking and decision coaching, through planning cycles (annual/quarterly), to execution support (OKRs, QBRs). You've identified the exact moments of strategic breakdown—8 months post-offsite, when reality hits, when the McKinsey partners are gone—where Luna provides immediate access versus waiting weeks.

The archetypal pain points are deeply understood from consulting experience: vague strategy masquerading as execution problems, feature factories that lost sight of what matters, founder bottlenecks at scale, leaders drowning in detail while missing strategic hits. These aren't hypothetical—they're lived patterns you've repeatedly solved.
reflective_summary.thought_prompt: When you picture a leader opening Luna during one of those "hard to be around, hard to tolerate" moments of strategic overwhelm—maybe it's 10pm after a brutal day of firefighting—what's the first thing Luna should show or ask that makes them think "she gets it, and she's got me"?
opportunities_for_enrichment: The "drop it and forget it until you're ready" promise is compelling, but what does Luna do with those fragments while leaders are away? Does she proactively synthesize patterns, flag contradictions, or prepare questions? Defining Luna's "background processing" behavior could strengthen the always-thinking positioning and create delightful moments of "she noticed something I didn't."

The consulting precedent is rich with pattern recognition—after working with multiple leaders through these archetypal challenges, what are the early warning signals that strategy is drifting? Could Luna develop a "health check" capability that surfaces leading indicators before leaders hit the 8-month panic moment, shifting from reactive rescue to proactive guidance?

output.vision: Every leader has access to a strategic thinking partner who's always there—making sense of the chaos, clarifying what matters, and helping them lead with confidence through the lonely heavy lifting of strategic leadership.
output.strategy: Luna democratizes continuous strategic partnership by being the "always-on" thinking companion that leaders drop fragments into, helping them clarify muddy strategy, bridge the execution gap, and maintain strategic coherence through the messy reality between planning cycles—delivering the value of a McKinsey partner at a fraction of the cost and with immediate availability.

traces.claudeThoughts:
The emergent themes reveal a business addressing a fundamental gap in strategic leadership support. The core insight isn't about better planning tools—it's about the loneliness and chaos of strategic leadership, and the inaccessibility of the type of continuous partnership that only expensive consultants currently provide.

The "continuous strategic companion" theme is the strongest differentiator and should anchor the vision. This isn't about replacing planning cycles or competing with McKinsey on big transformations—it's about being present in all the moments between: the 8-month post-offsite reality check, the Sunday night prep for Monday's decision, the quiet realization that the team is lost despite having "great strategy on paper."

The strategy should focus on the clarity bottleneck rather than trying to be everything to everyone. The insight that executives "NEVER need a new strategy—they just need help clarifying the one they have" is profound. Luna's role is chaos reduction, not strategy creation. This positions her as a thinking partner for the "lonely heavy lifting" rather than an automation tool.

For objectives, I'm drawing from the specific patterns identified: the reactive leader archetype who can't "make time" for strategy, the execution gap between planning and performance, and the democratization of strategic partnership. The objectives should reflect building toward that continuous companion model while proving value in the artifact moments that leaders already recognize (board meetings, QBRs, offsites).

The language throughout emphasizes their authentic voice: "muddy strategy," "feature factory," "reality punched you in the face," "tap on for continuous value." This isn't corporate consulting speak—it's the real experience of strategic leadership.

fragments.content:
**Growth-Stage Leadership Strategy Development Gap**

The document identifies a specific market opportunity for companies at 8-15M ARR or 60-150 staff who have transitioned from founder-led startups to needing formalized strategy. These organizations face a critical inflection point where founder-embedded knowledge must be extracted, clarified, and made executable across teams. The need is not for external consultants to create strategy, but for tools that help leadership teams articulate and refine the strategy already in founders' heads, then communicate it coherently for execution without constant founder involvement.

**AI-Assisted Strategy Tool Market Landscape**

The market scan reveals three main categories: (1) product roadmap platforms with embedded AI copilots (Aha!, Jira, Productboard) targeting mid-senior PMs; (2) dedicated "AI strategy consultant" tools (Xavier AI) generating McKinsey-style decks for founders and consultants; (3) enterprise planning platforms (Anaplan) focused on scenario modeling for finance/ops teams. Additionally, consulting-adjacent AR tools, general LLM templates, and indie startup idea validators exist. The space is early, fragmented, and most tools focus on either product strategy execution or rapid deck generation rather than deep strategic co-creation.

**Longitudinal Strategy Coach vs. One-Off Deck Generators**

Current tools predominantly offer transactional, one-time outputs (decks, roadmaps, plans) rather than ongoing strategic partnership. The envisioned product would function as an "expert decision coach" working with leaders over time, understanding their business context, extracting founder knowledge, and continuously refining vision, strategy, objectives, and execution principles. This would evolve from a single leader preparing for an annual offsite into a multi-user platform tracking quarterly strategy evolution, generating business review artifacts, and driving planning cycles—fundamentally different from existing point-in-time generation tools.

**Strategy Execution Platform Evolution Path**

The vision describes a clear product evolution from single-user, one-off strategy preparation tool to a comprehensive multi-user, multi-organization strategy and operations platform. Initial use case targets individual leaders preparing for strategy offsites, then expands to track quarterly strategy changes, generate monthly/quarterly business reviews, drive planning cycles, and support the meetings and mechanisms around strategic execution. This represents a land-and-expand business model moving from personal productivity to organizational infrastructure.

**Current Market Gap Analysis**

The competitive analysis reveals that existing tools fall into distinct, non-overlapping categories: WorkBoard and OnePlan focus on strategy execution assuming strategy is already clear; Xavier AI and similar tools generate one-off strategic artifacts; enterprise planning tools model scenarios but don't facilitate strategic narrative development; consulting AR tools support workshops but aren't persistent platforms. No current offering combines deep strategic elicitation, longitudinal coaching, framework integration, and evolution into an operational platform for growth-stage executive teams.

**Integration of Strategic Frameworks and Execution Methodologies**

The proposed solution would integrate established strategic thinking models (Helmer's 7 Powers, Porter's 5 Forces) with performance measurement approaches (OKRs), wrapped in a simple framework for communicating strategy. This bridges the gap between high-level strategic concepts and operational execution, accommodating how leadership teams actually work. Current tools either focus on execution (WorkBoard, OnePlan) without deep strategic elicitation, or generate strategy content without ongoing execution integration.

**Target Customer Segmentation and Positioning**

The analysis distinguishes between product managers (served by Aha!, Productboard), founders/light consultants (served by Xavier AI, indie tools), enterprise finance/ops teams (served by Anaplan), and identifies an underserved segment: executive teams at growth-stage companies (8-15M ARR, 60-150 staff) and similar teams within larger organizations. These customers need more than execution tools but less than full consulting engagements—they need facilitated strategic thinking that respects their existing knowledge while helping articulate and operationalize it.

**Expert-Backed AI vs. Pure Automation**

The document emphasizes a hybrid model where an AI agent is continuously improved by a team of veteran digital product strategy consultants behind the scenes. This contrasts with pure LLM-based tools that rely solely on prompt engineering and general models. The approach suggests that genuine strategic insight requires domain expertise embedded in the product, not just sophisticated language generation—positioning the tool as expert-augmented rather than consultant-replacing.

**Technology Stack and Product Architecture Patterns**

Most existing tools rely on general-purpose LLMs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini) with prompt engineering, RAG, and domain-specific scoring logic. Enterprise platforms add proprietary data pipelines, vector search, and agent frameworks connecting strategy to company metrics. The document suggests emerging patterns around MCP-enabled environments, RAG-based agents pulling from client documents, and "AI workforce" platforms that can be configured as bespoke strategy assistants—indicating the technical building blocks exist but haven't been assembled for the executive strategy coach use case.

**The Reactive Leader Archetype**

The target user is characterized by being "100% in reactive mode" - not because they're incompetent but because "they don't have time" to manage strategy proactively. Strategy and planning "comes around a few times a year and it's always a huge scramble." These leaders are juggling chaos, drowning in everyday decisions, dealing with employee expectations, and often become bottlenecks as organizations scale. They include founders at high-growth startups, VP-level executives at mid-market companies, and division leaders - essentially "any leader who needs a collaborative partner to wrangle strategy with them."

**Strategy Execution Gap: Planning to Performance**

The conversation identified that "getting the strategy done is just the beginning, and execution is everything." Luna bridges the gap between planning cycles and ongoing execution through integration with operational cadences. This includes compatibility with OKRs for performance measurement and automation of 75% of Monthly and Quarterly Business Reviews, leaving leaders to focus on "the polished narrative." The value isn't just in creating strategy documents but in maintaining strategic coherence through the messy reality of execution over time.

**Continuous Strategic Companion vs. Point-in-Time Tools**

Luna is positioned as a "longitudinal strategy coach" rather than a one-off deck generator. The core insight is that strategy isn't a discrete event but an ongoing process. Leaders can drop "snippets, parts, memos, or just random and seemingly unconnected information" into Luna continuously, and she helps make sense of the chaos over time. This contrasts with traditional consulting (McKinsey partners who leave after the offsite) or static planning tools. Luna is "always there, always thinking, and always ready to move you forward" - available in the critical moments 8 months after the offsite when "all you can remember is the sweet taste of aspiration before reality punched you in the face."

**Strategic Leadership Failure Patterns**

The conversation surfaced specific recurring scenarios: (1) Leaders blame team capability when actually the strategy is vague and priorities shift constantly - "that's a leadership problem"; (2) Great strategy exists but culture posters are "decorations" while teams are "completely lost sight of what actually matters"; (3) Founder-led orgs where founders become bottlenecks at scale, "exhausted" and "carrying ALL the responsibility" because "how they worked before isn't working at their current scale." Additional triggers include feeling exposed at board meetings with nothing new to say, key people leaving, missing more than hitting, and big initiatives "chewing resources but seemingly not going in the right direction."

**Clarity as the Core Strategic Bottleneck**

A critical insight emerged: executives "NEVER need (or want) a new strategy—they just need help clarifying the one they have." The strategy is "muddy, murky, and mixed up with all of the other chaos they're dealing with." Common manifestations include: teams thinking strategy is fine but execution is failing (actually the strategy is vague and direction changes constantly); having great strategy on paper but teams lost in "feature factory" velocity; and decision paralysis when choosing between "10 great options" with the instinct to "do it all." Luna's role is "chaos reduction" and helping with "the lonely heavy lifting of strategic leadership."

**Democratizing Strategic Partnership**

Luna addresses an accessibility gap in strategic support. The conversation highlighted that consulting partners at firms like McKinsey provide exactly this type of help - being there in critical moments, providing outside perspective, decision coaching, making strategy actionable - but "most people can't afford that luxury." By the time you "schedule the meeting and get lunch together, you're two weeks out, and it's too late." Luna makes this level of strategic partnership available continuously and affordably, serving as the "steady hand" that expensive consultants provide but at a fraction of the cost and with immediate availability.

**Flexible Engagement Model: Artifacts and Conversations**

The interaction model is deliberately flexible - "different strokes for different folks." Leaders engage with Luna both through specific artifacts (board decks, OKR refreshes, team offsite prep, Monthly/Quarterly Business Reviews) and freeform strategic thinking. The key is that leaders can "engage as often and for as long as they like" with the "tap on" for continuous value. Luna provides multiple types of support: decision coaching when stuck between options, making strategy actionable and motivating, providing outside-in perspective for "forest for the trees" moments, and "joining the dots" on seemingly unconnected information.
